political reform background and reference materials. However,
we are unable to provide a formal opinion with respect to the
request set forth in your letter regarding the Conflict of
Interest Code.

Honorable Commissioners:

Pursuant to Government Code Section 31(a) all references herein are to the Governance Code unless other code indicated. Request is hereby made on behalf the Board of Supervisors of the County of San Diego for a formal opinion with respect to the Board's duties in disposing of the north one-half of certain [illegible] property.

WRITTEN FACTS:

There is with a the City of San Diego a small parcel of property situate Lincoln's Way on the San Diego Bay. It is not part of San Diego, but by is claimed by the City of San Diego. [illegible] POLITICAL QUESTIONS
PERTAINING

1. Who is the person or firm most directly and actively engaged in the development of ERTS as a system, and in the negotiation of inter-agency agreements, and in any Memoranda of Understanding that have been executed?

2. How should the value of such a system be evaluated?

Mr. William Schwartz of this office and Mr. Tom Gould of your office in a letter of July 17, 1970, raised the question of the value of the dollar. Please address in financial terms the question of the understanding of a study. Can you furnish a working paper? [illegible] ERTS as it is planned.

SUBJECT

SAN DIEGO STADIUM DIRECTORS' AREA

PURPOSE

"To establish goals, long for all uses of all recreational and other purposes in the
Directors' Area as in the San Diego area."

POLICY

The Directors' Area was developed for use by the San Diego City Council, County
Planning Authority, School Districts and the San Diego Stadium Authority and their
guests, to provide access to the Stadium for persons who can aid the growth
and promotion of the City and County of San Diego. Specifically, the
following individuals during their terms of office will be entitled to use the
Directors' Area:

Mayor and Councilmen of the City of San Diego                    (5)

Board of Supervisors and Chief Administrative
Officer of the County of San Diego                              (4)

San Diego Stadium Authority Board of Directors                  (0)

City Attorney                                                   (1)

City Manager                                                    (2)

Each "essential" person in (1) above for (a) reserved parking lot per
car, and (B) to use of the operational area. Each essential person will
be issued two (2) tickets to the operational area and one (1) additional
West (3) patio/box parking lot ticket. (2) Each such person is entitled
to no more than scheduled parking lot a total when use tickets.
When using the Stadium as an invited guest and access to the unreserved area
has been disallowed, he shall be provided an 8 hour or daily available
rate when available. In the event the use is not available, an alternative
civic guests such as drug affiliates, sportscasters, major merchants, State
officials and others of equal status. All such persons use the directors'
Box E. All guests parking as an alternate lot.

Adopted by Resolution No. [illegible] on [illegible]
Amended by Resolution No. 209-84 7/17/84
Amended by Resolution No. 211379 6/20/84

[illegible]

sections.question: Are complimentary tickets provided to members of the San Diego Board of Supervisors and County Administrative Officer regarded as gifts under the Political Reform Act, and how should they be valued?
sections.conclusion: Complimentary tickets provided under the city's official policy to officials in connection with their official duties may not constitute gifts subject to the Act's disclosure requirements, provided the tickets are used in relation to official government functions and do not create conflicts of interest.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether complimentary tickets provided to county and city officials for sporting events at a city-owned stadium constitute reportable gifts under the Political Reform Act, and the proper valuation methodology for such gifts.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that complimentary tickets provided pursuant to an official city policy may not constitute reportable gifts if used in connection with official duties and governmental purposes, though valuation at fair market value is appropriate if disclosure is required.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant portions illegible or difficult to parse. Multiple pages appear to discuss related matters (parking tickets, disclosure requirements under Section 67347) but connection to main question is unclear. The conclusion and reasoning are fragmented due to text quality issues. No clear formal conclusion section identified; synthetic conclusion based on available context.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are complimentary tickets provided to members of the San Diego Board of Supervisors and County Administrative Officer regarded as gifts under the Political Reform Act, and how should they be valued?

CONCLUSION: Complimentary tickets provided under the city's official policy to officials in connection with their official duties may not constitute gifts subject to the Act's disclosure requirements, provided the tickets are used in relation to official government functions and do not create conflicts of interest.

embedding.summary: An FPPC response to Deputy County Counsel regarding whether complimentary tickets to stadium events provided to county and city officials under a San Diego city policy constitute reportable gifts and how to value them under the Political Reform Act.
